parts: You are a helpful senior React engineer.
How do I properly clean up a useEffect that subscribes to a WebSocket? My component keeps trying to update state after unmount and React yells at me about memory leaks.
Great question. The key is to return a cleanup function from your useEffect. Here's the canonical pattern:
What if the effect depends on a prop? Do I close and reopen the socket every time the prop changes?

Exactly — include the prop in the dependency array. React will run the cleanup from the previous render, then re-run the effect with the new prop value. It feels wasteful but it's the correct, predictable behavior. If reconnecting is expensive, consider debouncing the prop or lifting the socket into a ref and managing it manually.
That makes sense, thanks. One more thing — should I also abort in-flight fetches?

Yes! Use AbortController. Create a controller inside the effect, pass its signal to fetch, and call controller.abort() in the cleanup. This both cancels the network request and prevents the .then() callback from running after unmount.
My sourdough starter has been bubbling but the dough barely rose overnight. House temp is around 68F. What am I doing wrong?

68F is on the cool side for sourdough fermentation. A few things to try: First, find a warmer spot — near the oven with the light on, or in the oven with just the pilot light, is usually around 75-78F. Second, give it more time: at 68F an overnight bulk might need 10-14 hours instead of the usual 8. Third, check your starter maturity — a starter that's only a week old may not be strong enough yet. You want it to double within 4-6 hours of feeding at room temp.
It's about two weeks old. I feed it 1:1:1 once a day with bread flour.

Two weeks is borderline — it can take 3-4 weeks for a starter to really stabilize. Try feeding twice a day for the next few days, and switch to a 1:5:5 feeding ratio the night before you bake. That gives the culture more food and a longer, more acidic fermentation. Also: when you mix the dough, use water at around 80F to give the yeast a head start.

Let me know how the next bake goes. The other thing worth checking: your flour. Cheap all-purpose flour has lower protein content and won't develop gluten as well, which means less gas retention. Switch to bread flour (12-13% protein) and you'll get noticeably better oven spring.

messages.content: I'm planning a 10-day trip to Japan in November. First time. Want to split between Tokyo, Kyoto, and maybe one off-the-beaten-path place. Got any tips?

Nice. November is peak autumn-foliage season, so book accommodations ASAP. Suggested split: 4 nights Tokyo, 3 nights Kyoto, 2 nights somewhere like Kanazawa or Takayama (both reachable by shinkansen + limited express). Save the last night for Tokyo near your departure airport.
Is the JR Pass worth it?

Honestly, with the October 2023 price hike (~50% increase), the nationwide JR Pass is hard to justify for a single Tokyo-Kyoto round trip. Break-even is around 3+ long-distance trips. Better to buy individual shinkansen tickets, or look at regional passes (JR Kansai Area Pass, JR East Pass) if your itinerary stays within one region.
What about food? Anything I shouldn't miss?

Where to even start. In Tokyo: tsukemen at Rokurinsha in Tokyo Station, monjayaki in Tsukishima, sushi breakfast at Toyosu outer market (skip the touristy inner market). In Kyoto: kaiseki dinner at least once, yudofu near Nanzen-ji, matcha parfaits in Gion. In Kanazawa: kaiten sushi at Mori Mori for ultra-fresh Sea of Japan seafood. Skip 'robot restaurant' type tourist traps in Shinjuku.
Should I get a pocket WiFi or a SIM?

Pocket WiFi is more convenient if you have multiple devices or travel companions. Sakura Mobile and Ninja WiFi are reliable. Order ahead and pick up at the airport. If you're solo and only need phone data, an eSIM (Ubigi, Airalo) is cheaper and you skip the pickup counter entirely.
Day trips from Tokyo worth doing?

Kamakura (1 hr, great Buddha and temples + beach vibe). Nikko (2 hrs, ornate Toshogu shrine and mountain scenery). Hakone (2 hrs, onsen town with Mt Fuji views on clear days). Yokohama is closer (30 min) and has the best Chinatown in Japan and a fantastic cup noodle museum.
Random: do I need to bring cash or is it card everywhere now?

Cash is still king at smaller restaurants, temples, rural areas, and vending machines that take coins. Suica/Pasmo IC cards cover most transit and convenience stores. Bring a few hundred USD, exchange at the airport, and withdraw more at 7-Eleven ATMs as needed. Credit cards are accepted at department stores and mid-range+ restaurants but don't count on them everywhere.

messages.text: Do you think free will is compatible with determinism? I keep going back and forth on this.

This is one of those questions where the framing matters more than the answer. The compatibilist tradition (Hume, Dennett, Frankfurt) argues that free will is not the ability to have done otherwise under identical conditions, but rather the ability to act according to your own desires and reasons without external coercion. Under that definition, determinism isn't a threat — it's a precondition. Your actions are caused by your character, and your character is something you (slowly, partially) shape. The incompatibilist objection is that this is a bait-and-switch: that's not what anyone means by 'real' free will.
But if my character was itself caused by things outside my control — genes, upbringing, random quantum fluctuations — then I didn't really choose it either.

That's the regress objection, and it's a strong one. Compatibilists respond in a few ways. One: 'ultimate' origination is incoherent — even God's choices would either be caused (by God's nature) or random, and neither gives you the kind of free will you're asking for. So the demand for uncaused choice is a category error. Two: responsibility doesn't require uncaused choice; it requires the right kind of causal chain — one that goes through the agent's reasoning. We hold responsible agents who act from their own reasons, even if those reasons have causal antecedents.
What about libertarian free will — the idea that there's some kind of agent-causation that breaks the causal chain?

Libertarian free will (in the metaphysical sense, not the political one) is historically popular but philosophically embattled. The core problem: if your choice isn't determined by prior states of the universe, then by what? If it's random, that's not agency either — randomness isn't freedom, it's just a different kind of unfreedom. If it's determined by 'you' in some non-physical sense, you've just pushed the question back: what determines the nature of that agent? Some philosophers (e.g., Timothy O'Connor, Randolph Clarke) defend event-causal libertarianism with elaborate theories of agent causation, but most find the position mysterious.

Looks like you sent an empty message — happy to continue if you have a follow-up. One thread worth pulling: even if metaphysical free will doesn't exist, the practical experience of deliberation is real and unavoidable. You can't help but reason about what to do, and that reasoning itself is part of the causal chain that produces your action. So in a pragmatic sense, your choices are causally efficacious even if they aren't metaphysically 'free'.
That pragmatic framing actually resolves a lot of my anxiety about it. But does it have any practical consequences? Like for criminal justice?

Yes, and this is where compatibilism pays its rent. If responsibility is about responsive-to-reasons and shaped-by-character, then criminal justice should focus on deterrence, rehabilitation, and incapacitation rather than retribution. Hard determinists (e.g., Gregg Caruso, Derk Pereboom) go further and argue we should abandon the retributive framework entirely. Both views converge on similar policy: less punishment-for-punishment's-sake, more evidence-based rehabilitation. The disagreement is mostly about what we call it.
Last question: where should I read more? Primary sources.

Start with: Hume, 'An Enquiry Concerning Human Understanding' (Section VIII) for classical compatibilism. P.F. Strawson, 'Freedom and Resentment' (1962) for the reactive-attitudes turn. Harry Frankfurt, 'Alternate Possibilities and Moral Responsibility' (1969) for the principle of alternate possibilities and Frankfurt cases. Daniel Dennett, 'Elbow Room' and 'Freedom Evolves' for a modern compatibilist synthesis. Derk Pereboom, 'Living Without Free Will' for the hard-incompatibilist case. Galen Strawson, 'The Impossibility of Moral Responsibility' for a sharp regress argument. Each is short enough to read in a sitting and they argue with each other beautifully.

messages.content: I keep getting 'coroutine was never awaited' warnings in my async Python code. What does that actually mean and how do I track down the offending call?

It means you created a coroutine object (by calling an async function) but never scheduled it to run. The coroutine gets garbage-collected without ever executing its body, and Python warns you. Common causes: (1) calling an async function without await, like `fetch()` instead of `await fetch()`; (2) passing a coroutine to something that expects a regular function (like a callback); (3) storing the coroutine in a variable and forgetting to await it before the function returns. The traceback in the warning usually points at the coroutine's __init__ site, which is the function definition, not the call site — that's the confusing part. To find the actual call site, enable debug mode with `asyncio.get_event_loop().set_debug(True)` or run with `PYTHONASYNCIODEBUG=1`, which gives a much better traceback pointing at where the coroutine was created.
That debug tip is gold, thanks. Side question: what's the difference between asyncio.create_task and asyncio.ensure_future?

In modern Python (3.7+), `asyncio.create_task(coro)` is the preferred API — it schedules the coroutine on the running loop and returns a Task you can await or cancel. `asyncio.ensure_future(coro)` is the older, more general API: it accepts coroutines, futures, or awaitables and wraps them appropriately. For most code you want `create_task`. Use `ensure_future` only when you need to handle the generic awaitable case (rare).
